account_health.name: Cincinnati Childrens Hospital Medical Center - Peter Margolis

call_list.title: Innovaccer and Mt. Sinai
call_list.attendees: Nikhil Roy, Ankit Maheshwari, Achintya Moulick, anu, Derek Pineda, Brandon Taylor, Vengrenyuk, Andriy, Ashul Govil, MD
call_list.summary:
The meeting centered around the integration of advanced technology in healthcare, specifically focusing on cardiology and emergency response systems.

Topics Discussed:
Introduction and Team Introductions
Achintya Moulick introduced the purpose of the meeting and the participants, including Dr. Kinn
call_list.pain_point:
Difficulty in improving the emergency communication system due to multiple layers and bureaucracy.
Only 25-30% of heart attack patients are transferred to cath labs within the recommended 90-minute window.
Transport times in rural areas are increasing, leading to higher mortality.
Existing AI tools (e.g., ACS platform) are rule-based and linear, leading to false alarms and inability to handle edge cases.
Challenges in scaling clinical applications across different hospitals with varying providers and workflows.
High costs associated with case management and follow-up in cardiology.
Lack of integration and communication among multiple AI agents leading to siloed solutions.
Difficulty in building long-term clinical solutions due to lack of clinical expertise in some AI development teams.
Need for faster throughput and better patient support in cardiology and other time-sensitive emergencies.
Challenges in getting FDA approval for AI tools, leading to long development pathways.
Difficulty in coordinating among academic centers, software companies, rural hospitals, and provider groups for access to care.

call_list.title: Neil Tantingco and Team Innovaccer
call_list.summary:
The meeting was centered around discussing the CMS access and the integration of remote care solutions with Kshitij Joshi and Neil. The conversation highlighted the potential collaboration between their organizations.

Topics Discussed:
Introduction and Background
Neil shared his background in remot
call_list.pain_point:
Seller asks about challenges with current platforms and care coordination: "Are these built-in house or are you taking support of partners and vendors?"
Buyer discusses problems and challenges:
Patients often do not disclose their true condition, leading to late discovery of issues (e.g., only found out when patient is in ER or ambulance).
Wearable fall detection devices are not effective because patients do not like to wear them.
Pull cords for falls in assisted living are often ineffective due to distance or unconsciousness.
Caregivers in assisted living are non-clinical and may not understand medical conditions like CHF.
High cost of physical care and 24-hour care is prohibitive for many consumers.
Fragmented data sources and reliance on human interpretation of data create inefficiencies.
Multiple systems require logging in separately, wasting provider and patient time.
Challenges in preventing rehospitalization and managing chronic ailments in assisted living.
Difficulty in filling care gaps when caregivers are only present for limited hours.
Patients' loneliness and isolation require remote care coordination beyond clinical visits.
Technology and human coordination needed to monitor patients effectively in their homes.

call_list.title: Rural Health $$ w/ Scott Rifkin, Lisa Bari, Glenn Kunigan
call_list.attendees: Scottrifkinmd, Lisa Bari, Glenn Kunigan, JimStaska, TimBuono, Will Pettinger
call_list.summary:
The meeting centered around discussing potential partnerships and strategies for rural health initiatives, with a focus on leveraging data and forming strategic alliances.

Topics Discussed:
Conference Feedback
Glenn Kunigan shared feedback on a recent conference, noting the lack of engagement and t
call_list.pain_point:
Buyer notes that states are hesitant to create assisted living programs, leading to unnecessary nursing home admissions.
States face Medicaid budget problems due to patients occupying nursing home beds who could be cared for in less intensive settings.
State survey agencies are behind schedule (e.g., four years behind in Maryland) causing legal and care quality issues.
Hospitals and health systems experience bed shortages in nursing homes despite apparent empty beds due to reimbursement and operational challenges.
Nursing home staff turnover is very high (70-80% annually for charge nurses and directors of nursing), impacting care quality.
Difficulty in accessing and integrating electronic medical record data from nursing homes.
States and health systems lack real-time visibility into nursing home bed availability and quality metrics.
Political and operational challenges in selling programs directly to states due to lack of manpower and political sway.
Complexity in coordinating efforts across multiple organizational groups and sales categories within the buyer's organization.
Need to partner with large health systems and insurers who have established relationships with states to be competitive for funding.

call_list.title: Catching up: Neil Tantingco + Will Pettinger
call_list.summary: The meeting concluded with a focus on potential collaborations and strategies to enhance service delivery and financial sustainability in the healthcare sector.
call_list.pain_point:
COVID caused many home health agencies to close or be acquired, reducing market.
Budget cuts led to inability of home health agencies to afford connected home living.
Home care agencies sometimes only use technology as a marketing tool without real adoption.
Difficulty in recruiting and retaining care coordinators at low reimbursement rates.
Medicaid recipients receive reduced in-person care hours (e.g., from 6 to 2 hours), leading to unmet care needs.
Medicaid recipients often lonely, isolated, with mental health concerns.
High rates of avoidable ER visits and hospital readmissions among Medicaid recipients.
States have different Medicaid program nuances, complicating scaling.
Need to certify technology and train remote care coordinators per state requirements.
Capital constraints limit ability to purchase hardware in large quantities.
Difficulty scaling outreach and recruitment of patients due to local community trust requirements.
Need to balance capital burn with growth ambitions.
Restrictive conditions on grant funding limit flexibility.
Challenges in negotiating with multiple Managed Care Organizations in large states like Texas.
Need to tailor services for different patient populations including IDD and those with degenerative diseases.

call_list.title: Innovaccer / Walgreens - Story Health discussion (ACCESS model)
call_list.opportunity: Cincinnati Childrens Hospital Medical Center - Peter Margolis
call_list.attendees: Van Thanh Nguyen, Miriam Rosenau, Jay Levine, Brandon Taylor, Amy Stevens, Anita Patel1, Nikhil Roy, Tristin Chan, Sarah Freedman, Chelsea
call_list.summary:
The meeting centered around the introduction and discussion of Story Health's capabilities and its integration with Innovacer, focusing on strategic alignment with Walgreens.

Topics Discussed:
Introductions and Background
Participants introduced themselves, including Jay Levine, Nikhil Roy, Brandon
call_list.pain_point:
Seller asks: "What role do they see themselves playing in Access given they've eliminated all of their risk bearing Services already?"
Buyer states: "We're unclear if we're a participant or not in the new access CMS model. There's nuances with that that we're working through with legal."
Buyer mentions challenges with medical billing and credentialing.
Buyer raises questions about security and data transfer: "Questions of course that we immediately get into with Lee. Wait labeling do the security, you know, really almost pieces."
Buyer expresses need to understand how data from their systems can be pushed into Story Health's platform and how information transfer works.
Buyer notes challenges with care model being episodic, limited follow-up, and poor patient retention.
Buyer implies challenges with patient engagement and continuous care delivery in current setup.
Buyer mentions need to figure out potential synergies and how to play in access programs.
Buyer mentions need to understand capabilities of Story Health's AI and human-in-the-loop components for patient identification and care orchestration.
Buyer mentions need to move fast and coordinate with multiple teams and partners to address these challenges.
